summary: The primary auscultatory finding in patients with post-tuberculosis bronchiectasis includes late inspiratory crackles due to airway obstruction or secretions within dilated bronchioles. These are often bilateral but may be more prominent at end-inspiration reflecting underlying structural lung damage secondary to previous tuberculosis infection. Late inspiratory refers specifically when these breath sounds occur later than normal vesicular breath sounds typically heard throughout respiration cycle indicating potential for fluid accumulation or narrowed passages causing delayed sound transmission through lungs upon inhalation phase compared usual early expirations where such issues might present differently if they were occurring earlier rather than latter part of respiratory event sequence observed clinically here based available data set analyzed thoroughly above.
